title: FDIC vs NCUA: What's the Difference and Is Your Money Safe?
excerpt: Confused about FDIC vs NCUA? Learn who insures banks and credit unions, what accounts are covered, and how to keep your savings protected.
content:
If you have ever wondered whether your money is safer at a bank or a credit union, you are really asking about FDIC vs NCUA insurance. The good news is that both systems are designed to protect depositors. The confusing part is that many people do not know which agency covers which institution, what the limits are, or what kinds of accounts count.

In simple terms, the FDIC insures deposits at banks, while the NCUA insures deposits at federally insured credit unions. For most everyday savers, the protection is very similar. Both generally cover up to $250,000 per depositor, per institution, per ownership category.

This guide explains the real difference between FDIC and NCUA coverage, what is protected, what is not protected, and how to make sure your savings stay within the insurance rules. If you are building an [emergency fund](/blog/emergency-fund-how-much-to-save) or comparing a [high-yield savings account vs traditional savings](/blog/high-yield-savings-vs-traditional-savings-2026), this is one of the most useful safety checks you can make.

## What is the FDIC?

The FDIC stands for the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation. It is an independent U.S. government agency that protects depositors if an FDIC-insured bank or savings association fails. According to the FDIC, insurance is automatic when you open a covered deposit account at an FDIC-insured institution. You do not buy it separately or sign up for it.

FDIC insurance covers deposit accounts such as:

- Checking accounts
- Savings accounts
- Money market deposit accounts
- Certificates of deposit, or CDs

The standard coverage amount is $250,000 per depositor, per insured bank, per ownership category.

## What is the NCUA?

The NCUA stands for the National Credit Union Administration. It administers the National Credit Union Share Insurance Fund, which protects deposits at federally insured credit unions. Like FDIC coverage, NCUA share insurance is automatic.

The NCUA uses the word share because credit unions are member-owned institutions, but from a saver's perspective the practical idea is the same. If your federally insured credit union fails, insured deposits are protected up to the legal limit.

NCUA coverage generally applies to:

- Share draft accounts, which work like checking accounts
- Share savings accounts
- Money market accounts at credit unions
- Share certificates, which are similar to CDs

The standard coverage amount is also $250,000, with separate rules for different ownership categories.

## FDIC vs NCUA: the short answer

If you want the quick answer, here it is:

- **FDIC** covers banks
- **NCUA** covers federally insured credit unions
- **Both** generally insure up to $250,000 per depositor, per institution, per ownership category
- **Both** are backed by the full faith and credit of the United States
- **Both** automatically cover eligible deposit accounts

For most people, the main difference is not the quality of the protection. It is the type of institution holding your money.

## Are credit unions FDIC insured?

No. Credit unions are not FDIC insured. If a credit union is federally insured, it is usually covered by the NCUA instead.

This is one of the most common banking questions because people often assume all federally protected deposits fall under the FDIC. That is not how it works. Banks and savings associations typically use FDIC insurance. Federally insured credit unions use NCUA insurance.

Some state-chartered credit unions may use private insurance instead of federal insurance, so it is smart to verify the type of coverage before opening an account. The safest move is to confirm that the credit union is federally insured through the NCUA.

## Is your money safer at a bank or a credit union?

For insured deposits within the coverage limits, both can be very safe. A federally insured bank and a federally insured credit union both offer strong government-backed protection for eligible deposit accounts.

That means if you are deciding where to keep your savings, the better question is often not safety but fit:

- Does the account pay a competitive interest rate?
- Are there monthly fees?
- Is customer service good?
- Do you prefer a bank or a member-owned credit union?
- Is the institution federally insured?

If your money is within the insurance limits, both types of institutions can be appropriate places for your [short-term savings goals](/blog/how-to-set-financial-goals) and emergency reserves.

## What accounts are covered?

FDIC and NCUA insurance both focus on deposit accounts, not investments. That means the following are usually covered when held at an insured institution:

- Checking
- Savings
- Money market deposit accounts
- CDs or share certificates
- Certain retirement deposit accounts, depending on ownership category

The exact account labels may vary between banks and credit unions, but the general purpose is similar.

## What is not covered?

This is where people sometimes get tripped up. Deposit insurance does **not** protect you from investment losses, market drops, or bad investment choices. It also does not cover every product sold by a bank or credit union.

Items that are generally **not** covered include:

- Stocks
- Bonds
- Mutual funds
- Annuities
- Life insurance products
- Crypto assets

For example, if you buy a mutual fund through your bank, that does not make it FDIC insured. If you open a brokerage account at a credit union partner, that does not make your investments NCUA insured. Deposit insurance protects deposits, not investments.

If you are investing for long-term growth, it helps to understand how this differs from brokerage protections like SIPC coverage. That matters when you [open a brokerage account](/blog/how-to-open-brokerage-account-beginners-guide) or start learning about [index funds for beginners](/blog/index-funds-for-beginners).

## How the $250,000 limit really works

The headline number is $250,000, but the full rule is more specific: per depositor, per institution, per ownership category.

That means the limit is not always just one flat total. Your coverage depends on how your accounts are titled.

### Example 1: one person, one bank

If you have:

- $50,000 in checking
- $100,000 in savings
- $75,000 in a CD

all in your name alone at the same FDIC-insured bank, those accounts are generally added together within the same ownership category. Your total would be $225,000, so all of it would be insured.

### Example 2: one person, too much in one ownership category

If that total were $310,000 at the same bank, $250,000 would generally be insured and $60,000 would be uninsured.

### Example 3: joint account coverage

If you and a partner share a joint account, joint-account rules can provide separate coverage from your individual accounts. That can increase total insured protection when accounts are structured correctly.

### Example 4: different institutions

If you have $250,000 at one insured bank and another $250,000 at a different insured bank, those limits generally apply separately because the institutions are different.

This is one reason some savers spread large cash balances across institutions instead of keeping everything in one place.

## Ownership categories matter more than most people realize

Both FDIC and NCUA coverage use ownership categories to determine insurance limits. Common categories include:

- Single accounts
- Joint accounts
- Certain retirement accounts
- Revocable trust or payable-on-death accounts

This matters because two accounts at the same institution may or may not be combined depending on how they are owned.

For example, your personal savings account and your IRA may not be treated the same way for insurance purposes. A joint account with your spouse is also different from an individual checking account in your name alone.

If you keep large balances, use the FDIC or NCUA insurance estimator tools before assuming you are fully covered.

## How to check whether your institution is insured

Never assume. Verify it.

To confirm coverage:

- Use the FDIC BankFind tool for banks
- Use the NCUA Credit Union Locator for credit unions
- Look for the official insurance sign on the website or in the branch
- Ask customer service directly if the institution is federally insured

This is especially important with fintech apps, cash management accounts, and newer online financial products. Some apps are not banks themselves. They may place funds with partner banks, and coverage can depend on how those accounts are structured.

## When FDIC vs NCUA matters most

For small and medium balances, the difference may not change much in your daily life. But it matters more when:

- You keep more than $250,000 in cash at one institution
- You are using multiple account owners or beneficiaries
- You are opening accounts for a business or trust
- You are deciding between a bank and a credit union for a large emergency fund or house down payment
- You are using a financial app and want to confirm where the money actually sits

If you are saving for a near-term purchase, protecting principal matters more than chasing slightly higher returns. That is why many people keep large cash goals in insured deposit accounts instead of investing them in the market.

## Should you choose a bank or a credit union?

Here is the practical answer: choose the institution that gives you the right combination of safety, convenience, fees, and rates.

A bank may be a better fit if you want:

- More branches or ATMs
- A strong app and digital tools
- A high-yield savings option
- Business banking services

A credit union may be a better fit if you want:

- A member-owned structure
- Lower fees in some cases
- Personalized service
- Competitive savings or loan rates

Insurance protection alone usually should not be the deciding factor, as long as the institution is federally insured and your balances stay within the rules.

## Common mistakes to avoid

### 1. Assuming all financial products at a bank are insured

They are not. Deposit insurance is for eligible deposit accounts, not investments.

### 2. Forgetting to add accounts together

Multiple accounts in the same ownership category at the same institution are often combined for insurance purposes.

### 3. Confusing a fintech app with a bank

Some apps work through partner banks. Verify where the money is actually held.

### 4. Ignoring private insurance at some credit unions

Not every credit union uses federal insurance. Check before depositing large amounts.

### 5. Keeping too much cash at one institution

If your cash balance grows above coverage limits, consider spreading funds across institutions or ownership categories where appropriate.

## Bottom line

When it comes to FDIC vs NCUA, the difference is mostly about **who** insures your deposits, not whether one system is clearly safer for ordinary savers. FDIC insurance protects deposits at insured banks. NCUA insurance protects deposits at federally insured credit unions. In both cases, eligible deposits are generally covered up to $250,000 per depositor, per institution, per ownership category.

If your goal is to keep your savings safe, the key steps are simple: confirm the institution is federally insured, understand which accounts are covered, and make sure your balances stay within the rules. Do that, and your money can be well protected whether you choose a bank or a credit union.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is NCUA as safe as FDIC?

For insured deposits at federally insured institutions, both provide strong government-backed protection. For most savers, the practical level of safety is very similar.

### Are credit unions safer than banks?

Both can be safe places for cash if they are federally insured and your balances stay within coverage limits. The better choice often depends on rates, fees, service, and convenience.

### Does FDIC or NCUA cover investments?

No. They cover eligible deposit accounts, not stocks, bonds, mutual funds, or other investment products.

### Can I have more than $250,000 insured?

Yes, in some cases. Coverage can increase across different ownership categories or separate insured institutions. The exact amount depends on how your accounts are structured.

### How do I know if my credit union is federally insured?

Check the institution's website, look for the official NCUA insurance sign, or verify it through the NCUA Credit Union Locator.

## Sources

- [FDIC: Deposit Insurance FAQs](https://www.fdic.gov/resources/deposit-insurance/faq/)
- [NCUA: Share Insurance Coverage](https://ncua.gov/consumers/share-insurance-coverage)
- [NCUA: Frequently Asked Questions About Share Insurance](https://ncua.gov/consumers/share-insurance-coverage/frequently-asked-questions-about-share-insurance)
